coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots based charge bridge between photoanode and electrocatalysts for efficiency water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xiao
Zhang, Zemin
Mei, Junfeng
Han, Dongyan
Xie, Mingzheng
Wang, Fangcong
Xie, Erqing
Han, Weihua
coredata.dc:description: Due to its earth abundant and excellent stability, metal oxide semiconductor attracts great attention in photoelectrochemical water splitting. However, its performance is restricted due to the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs even modified with electrocatalysts. In this work, carbon quantum dots are studied as the charge bridge to boost the charge transfer kinetics of cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi)/Fe2O3 photoanode at the photoanode/electrocatalyst interface. Our results show that carbon quantum dots significantly improve the charge transfer and charge separation efficiency of Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode. The photocurrent is substantially enhanced to 4.9 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) which is much higher than Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode 3.7 mA/cm2 in the same situation. These results provide a strategy to improve photoanode performance by facilitating the electrical contact at the photoanode/electrocatalyst interface with carbon quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hematite
Carbon quantum dots
Charge separation efficiency
Surface photovoltage spectrum
Water oxidation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U1531114 , 51572118 and 61404066 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (China, Grant No. lzujbky-2017-180 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic synthesis process of the composite C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode.

The microstructure of different photoanodes. Low and high resolution SEM images of Fe2O3 photoanode (a), CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode (b) and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode (c); TEM image of Fe2O3 (d), CQDs/Fe2O3 (e) and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 (f). The inset of d shows the HRTEM of α-Fe2O3.
The crystal phases and composition of different photoanodes. The XRD patterns of Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes (a), Raman spectra of Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes (b).

The X-ray photoelectron spectra of the samples. Typical XPS survey spectra of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode (a). XPS high resolution Fe 2p (b), O 1s (c), C 1s (d), Co 2p (e) and P 2p (f) spectra of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode.

Optical performance of different photoanodes. (a) UV–visible absorption spectra of Fe2O3, Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes. (b) The optical band gap of Fe2O3, Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes.

Photoelectrochemical properties of different samples. LSV curves of Fe2O3 and Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes (a). LSV curves of Co-Pi/Fe2O3, C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes (b). IPCE (c) and ABPE (d) of Fe2O3, Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes.

The efficiency of different samples. The charge transfer efficiency (a) and separation efficiency (b) of Fe2O3, CQDs/Fe2O3, Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes. SPS of Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes. Electrochemical impedance spectra (d) of different photoanodes, the inset of d is RC (resistance and capacitance) circuit model.
Charge separation and transfer mechanism in the C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots based charge bridge between photoanode and electrocatalysts for efficiency water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Earth and Environmental Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to its earth abundant and excellent stability, metal oxide semiconductor attracts great attention in photoelectrochemical water splitting. However, its performance is restricted due to the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs even modified with electrocatalysts. In this work, carbon quantum dots are studied as the charge bridge to boost the charge transfer kinetics of cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi)/Fe2O3 photoanode at the photoanode/electrocatalyst interface. Our results show that carbon quantum dots significantly improve the charge transfer and charge separation efficiency of Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode. The photocurrent is substantially enhanced to 4.9 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) which is much higher than Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode 3.7 mA/cm2 in the same situation. These results provide a strategy to improve photoanode performance by facilitating the electrical contact at the photoanode/electrocatalyst interface with carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting has attracted more and more attention as a result of its huge potential in solving the increasing energy crisis by converting solar energy into transportable and storable hydrogen [1–5]. Due to its earth abundant and excellent stability, metal oxide semiconductor attracts great attention in PEC water splitting. However, its performance is restricted due to the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs even modified with electrocatalysts. Hematite (Fe2O3) is a metal oxide semiconductor which is widely studied. As theoretically promising photoanode material, Fe2O3 has been widely investigated because of its narrow energy band-gap and excellent light absorption [3,6,7]. However, we were disappointed by its experimental performance which is believed to be hindered by the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs inside or at the surface of Fe2O3 [8–11]. Generally, electrocatalysts were used to improve the charge transfer kinetics from Fe2O3 to electrolyte through boosting the oxygen reaction. Among them, cobalt phosphate (Co-Pi) is a promising candidate which shows effective water oxidation performance and high stability over time [12]. Despite the progress we have made on Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes, the solar-to-hydrogen efficiency is still limited by the poor charge transfer kinetics between Fe2O3, Co-Pi and the electrolyte. The charge transfer kinetics is believed to be determined by the electrical contact between Co-Pi and Fe2O3. However, electrocatalyst used Co-Pi is amorphous and the electrical contact to Fe2O3 is unfavorable for the charge transfer [13,14].

Xie and co-workers has reported TiO2-BiVO4 photoanode bridged with phosphate as charge transport path to optimize the transfer of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [15]. The photocurrent of the phosphate-bridged TiO2-BiVO4 photoanode was tripled at the applied voltage of 0.4 V compared with that without phosphate-bridge. But the stability of the phosphate bridge needs further improvement. Recently, carbon materials which are much more stable than phosphate come into the sight of researchers in this field. Zhang and co-workers claimed that reduced graphene oxide (rGO) could form an Ohmic contact with photoanode and thus facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [16]. Their results show that the rGO-ITO@Fe2O3 photoanode can be significantly enhanced up to 3.5 times than that of ITO@Fe2O3. The improved performance is attributed to the excellent physical/chemical performance of rGO (superb conductivity) which guarantee an equable distribution on photoanodes [16–20]. However, rGO is a sheet-like two-dimensional material. The active sites were easily covered by the rGO sheets and the oxygen evolution reaction will be hindered.

In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a charge bridge were used to facilitate the charge separation and charge transfer in Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes. CQDs which has similar physical and chemical properties of rGO, it may be a more promising candidate as the building blocks of such a charge transfer bridge. CQDs are expected to penetrate into the gaps between the electrolytes and the photoanodes to form favorable electrical contacts [13]. As expected, the PEC performance of the CQDs-modified Co-Pi/Fe2O3 (C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3) photoanode was significantly improved. The photocurrent at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) was enhanced from 3.7 to 4.9 mA/cm2. Hole scavenger measurements and surface photovoltage spectrum (SPS) analysis were used to study the charge transfer and charge separation efficiency. CQDs increased the charge separation efficiency increased from 41% to 49% and the charge transfer efficiency increased from 83% to 92% at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl).
The C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode was synthesized through the following procedures as diagramed in Fig. 1 .

First, Fe2O3 nanorod arrays were grown on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glasses (2.2 mm in thickness, >80% transmittance, 8 Ω per square, Nippon) by hydrothermal method [21]. In this procedure, a piece of FTO glass (0.8 × 1.5 cm2) was firstly cleaned with acetone, ethanol and deionized (DI) water for 10 min in each solution. The FTO glass which is cleaned was fixed on a mold and then put into an autoclave with 30 mL of solution containing 1.215 g of ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O, 99.0%, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, China) and 2.55 g of sodium nitrate (NaNO3, AR, Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory, China) and 50 μL hydrochloric (HCl, AR, Baiyin Liangyou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China). The autoclave was kept for 10 h in an oven for β-FeOOH nanorods growth at 100 °C. The β-FeOOH nanorods which have a yellow color layer were then cleaned with DI water to clear the residuum. After a two-step annealing process, i.e., 550 °C for 4 h and 800 °C for 15 min. A reddish-brown film consisted of Fe2O3 nanorods grown on FTO was achieved and directly used as photoanodes.

Secondly, CQDs were decorated onto the Fe2O3 nanorod arrays with a one-step electrochemical approach prior to Co-Pi electrocatalyst deposition [22]. The CQDs were prepared by electrochemical stripping [23]. The bias used in this experiment is 25 V between two graphite rods with a distance of 7.5 cm. The as-prepared CQDs solution was purified by centrifuge (11000 rpm) to remove large carbon particles. The Fe2O3 photoanode was immersed into 80 mL CQDs solution and treated at 90 °C for 3 h. The obtained CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode was washed with DI water, dry for 1 h and then annealed at 150 °C for 1 h.

Finally, C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode was achieved by electrodepositing Co-Pi onto the CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode with a photo-assisted electrodeposition process [24]. The electrodeposition process was conducted in a three electrode electrochemical cell using the photoanodes as working electrode, titanium plate as counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. The solution used in this process is a mixture containing 0.5 mM cobalt (II) nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, AR, Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd., China) in a 30-mL phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.9, Shanghai FangQi Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The deposition was performed at a constant voltage of 0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) under illumination (AM 1.5 G) for 200 s. Finally, the deposition samples were transferred to a vacuum environment and dried at 60 °C for 6 h. The Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes were also prepared in the same situation for comparison.

The microscopic morphology of the photoanode was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, TESCAN, VEGA3), and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai F30). Raman scattering spectra (Jobin-Yvon LabRam HR800 spectroscope, 532-nm line of Torus 50 mW diode-pumped solid-state laser) was used to study the composition of the samples. The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by using the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips, X'per pro, Cu kα 0.154056 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD XPS instrument, Al Kα source, hυ = 1486.6 eV) was used to analyze the surface electron configuration and chemical composition of the photoanodes. Photoluminescence (PL) characterization (ZLX-PL-I) of the samples were carried out to indicate the characteristics of CQDs. A spectrophotometer (HITACHI, U-3900H, Japan) was used to study optical absorption spectra of the photoanodes, operating in absorption mode and a normal incidence using a cleaned FTO substrate as reference.

The photoelectrochemical properties of the photoanode are measured by a three-electrode system. Simulated solar illumination was provided by a 550 W Xe lamp equipped with an AM 1.5 G filter and the incident photo intensity was calibrated to 100 mW cm−2 by a standard silicon solar cell. The aqueous solution of NaOH (1 M, pH = 13.6) was used as electrolyte. The photoanodes were used as working electrode, Ag/AgCl and a platinum gauze electrode as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. An electrochemical work station (RST5200, Zhengzhou Shiruisi Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used to test the photoelectrochemical performance and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The photocurrent was measured with a scan range of −0.4 V to 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and a scan rate of 50 mV/s. EIS measurements (0.01 Hz–100 kHz) were carried out at open-circuit voltage with a potential pulse of 7 mV in amplitude. Potentials with respect to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale were calculated using the relationship ERHE = EAg/AgCl + E0Ag/AgCl + 0.059 pH, where E0Ag/AgCl = 0.197 V at 25 °C for Ag/AgCl electrode (1 M KCl) and EAg/AgCl is the experimentally measured potential. Surface photovoltage was taken with a ZLX-PL-I SPM system with a lock-in amplifier. The practical oxygen generation rate of the photoanodes was measured by gas chromatography (Perfect Light, Lab solar-III AG).

Fig. 2 a shows an SEM image of the Fe2O3 nanorod (NR) arrays on FTO substrate and most of them grow vertically on the substrate. Upon the integration of CQDs and Co-Pi, the Fe2O3 nanorods are still uniform and without observable shape change (Fig. 2b and c). The TEM image (Fig. 2d) has provided a more direct comparison and confirmation of the nanorod structure. The Fe2O3 nanorods have uniform size and ordered shape with diameters of about 50–100 nm. The HRTEM shows the interplanar crystal spacing of 0.252 nm which is consistent with the (110) plane of Hematite Fe2O3 (Fig. 2d inset). After treated in CQDs solution at 90 °C for 3 h, CQDs were successfully and uniformly deposited onto the Fe2O3 surface (Fig. 2e). The diameters of the CQDs are about 3 nm as shown in Fig. S1. Other features of CQDs are in Supporting Information. Fig. 2f shows the typical TEM image of CQDs and amorphous Co-Pi supported on Fe2O3 nanorods. It is clear that Co-Pi is coated on Fe2O3 photoanode, while CQDs fill the gaps.

After XRD measurements, the crystal structure of the sample was cleared. In Fig. 3 a, the XRD peaks of Fe2O3 are in keeping with hematite Fe2O3 (JCPDS no. 33-0664) and SnO2 (JCPDS no. 41-1445). Because the Fe2O3 on FTO substrate is extremely thin, so the XRD signal is mainly from SnO2 on FTO glass. The weak peaks at 36.38° and 64.68° are believed to be introduced by hematite. There are no observable peaks from CQDs or Co-Pi catalysts due to their limited amount in the composite. In order to investigate the existence of CQDs, Raman spectrum was performed on pristine Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes as shown in Fig. 3b. The vibrational modes at 221, 243, 292, 407, 495, 604 and 1309 cm−1 belong to hematite Fe2O3. The peaks which are in keeping with the two A1g modes of hematite Fe2O3 are 221 and 495 cm−1, and the peaks which are in keeping with its Eg modes are 243, 292, 407, 604 and 1309 cm−1 [25,26]. The more intense peaks which are in keeping with the first order D (A1g symmetry) and G (E2g mode) bands of CQDs are 1315 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1. The D (A1g symmetry) band is generally believed to be caused by sp3 defects. While G (E2g mode) band is generally considered to be an in-plane vibration of an sp2 hybrid carbon atom [27,28]. And we can conclude from the intensity ratios of D and G band that there are many sp3 defects on the sp2 carbon networks of the sample, implying that CQDs are present.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface electron configuration and chemical composition of the photoanodes. Fig. 4 a shows a typical XPS survey spectra, which including the peaks from Fe, O, C, Co and P. Fig. 4b–f show the Fe 2p, O 1s, C 1s, Co 2p and P 2p core level XPS scanned at higher resolution conditions. The existence of Fe, O, C, Co and P elements were further identified. The peaks at binding energy of 710.9 eV and 724.3 eV are ascribed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, which confirm the existence of Fe3+ ions near the photoanode surface. Meanwhile, two satellite peaks of the Fe 2p main line are approximately observed at 719.0 and 733.2 eV. These satellites are most likely indicative of the presence of Fe3+ species. The symmetric peaks at 726.0 eV and 712.4 eV might be attributed to FeOOH. In addition, the peaks at 723.1 eV and 709.6 eV might own to Fe2+, due to the presence of defects (oxygen vacancies). This implies that the oxygen vacancies still exist after high temperature sintering, which is coincident with the n–type semiconductor hematite (Fig. 4b) [16]. Fig. 4c shows the two peaks located at 529.6 (corresponds to lattice oxygen) and 532.2 eV (hydroxyl group) of O1s. After the introduction of CQDs (Fig. 4d), the main peak at 284.6 eV (sp2 orbital) can be assigned to the CC bond. The peak at 285.8 and 288.0 eV can be indexed to the CO and OCO bonds, respectively, indicating the existence of CQDs [29]. The XPS peaks at 797.8 eV and 781.5 eV and followed by two satellite peaks at 786.8 eV and 804.1 eV (Fig. 4e) corresponds to the Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 spin-orbits in Co-Pi catalysts. With regard to P 2p spectra, a weak doublet lied at 128.9 and 133.1 eV can be indexed to P 2p 3/2 and P 2p 1/2 lines (Fig. 4f) [30]. Compared with the XRD spectra of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3, it further proves that Co-Pi catalysts is an amorphous structure. Combined with XPS, Raman, XRD and microstructure of the samples, it can be concluded that the C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode has been successfully achieved.

The optical properties of the photoanodes were investigated by using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. In Fig. 5 a, the Fe2O3 photoanode has obvious light absorption when the wavelength is less than 600 nm. After the integration of CQDs and Co-Pi catalysts, the light absorption spectra are almost keep the same despite a little increase in Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and decrease in C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3. The Kubelka-Munk function was used to estimate their optical bandgaps [31]. The Kubelka-Munk function can be descripted as: (αhυ) n = A (Eg-hυ), where υ is the frequency of light, h is Planck's constant, Eg is the bandgap, A is a constant and α is the absorption coefficient of the material. Fe2O3 is a typical indirect bandgap semiconductor. Hence, the value of n is 1/2. Through plotting the graph of (αhυ)1/2 versus hυ, the optical band gap of the sample can be deduced. As plotted in Fig. 5b, the bandgaps of Fe2O3, Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 are about the same value of 2.06 eV. It seems the integration of CQDs and Co-Pi catalysts has no significant effect on the optical bandgap and optical absorption of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 composite.

The photoelectrochemical properties of the photoanode are measured by a three-electrode system using an Electrochemical Work station. The electrolyte was 1 M NaOH aqueous solution (pH 13.6). Fig. 6 a shows the liner sweep voltammograms (LSV) of Fe2O3 photoanode under white-light illumination with AM 1.5 G. The Fe2O3 photoanode at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) has a photocurrent about 2.7 mA/cm2. The Fe2O3 photoanode has an excellent photo-stability in the photocurrent density over 600 s (Fig. S3). Our investigation shows that the annealing temperature is a key factor that determines the final PEC performance of the Fe2O3 photoanodes as discussed in Supporting Information. Despite the optimization by annealing process, the energy conversion efficiency of Fe2O3 is still limited due to the low mobility and short life time of photo-generated holes. Generally, Co-Pi catalysts were introduced to collect and store the photogenerated holes from Fe2O3 photoanode [8,9]. In this work, the photocurrent at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) was enhanced from 2.7 mA/cm2 to 3.7 mA/cm2 after Co-Pi decoration. However, the Co-Pi catalysts are generally in amorphous state. The electrical contact to Fe2O3 is not so good, which will hinder the charge transfer, and there is still a big development space. We improved the electrical contact by filling the Co-Pi and Fe2O3 gaps with conductive CQDs through a hydrothermal method. The decorated CQDs amount was controlled by the hydrothermal reaction time. The optimized reaction time has been found to be 3 h as shown in Fig. S6. The photocurrent at 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) can be increased to 4.9 mA/cm2 (Fig. 6b). Compared with Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode without CQDs (3.7 mA/cm2, 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl), the photocurrent has increased by 32%. Other photoelectrochemical properties can be found in the supporting information. It shows that both the CQDs and Co-Pi catalysts contribute to the enhanced photocurrent. The onset potential of photocurrent shifts to negative side by about 0.19 V (Fig. S8). The dark current plot appears a significant redox peak from Co-Pi catalysts at 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. S9) [30]. And the dark current of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode becomes larger than that of Fe2O3 photoanode. It shows that the Co-Pi catalysts has efficiently collected and stored the photo-generated holes from the Fe2O3 photoanode. It reduces the recombination of photocarriers which will show a lower photocurrent onset potential and higher water oxidation efficiency [13,32]. But the functions of the CQDs should be further studied.

Incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) is a commonly used indicator to study the photoelectrochemical efficiency of photoanode [33]. IPCE was tested at a bias voltage of 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). As shown in Fig. 6c, the C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode shows a visibly enhanced photoelectrochemical efficiency compared with Fe2O3 and Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes range from 350 to 650 nm. The introduction of CQDs and Co-Pi catalysts on the Fe2O3 photoanode leads to an increased IPCE value compared with Fe2O3 photoanode. The applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) was calculated (Fig. 6d), and the calculated value of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode is 0.36% at 1.04 V vs. RHE which has tripled that of Fe2O3 photoanode. The enhanced photoactivity of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode may be attributed to the co-modification effect of CQDs and Co-Pi [34].

In order to further study the photoelectrochemical properties of different photoanodes, the practical oxygen generation rate of the photoanodes was measured by gas chromatography at a bias voltage of 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2) in 1 M NaOH solution. The oxygen evolution rate of different photoanodes is shown in Fig. S11. Compared with Fe2O3 photoanode, the oxygen evolution of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode rate has increased.

The PEC performance of the photoanodes was codetermined by the light absorption, charge separation and charge transfer efficiencies [35,36]. From the light absorption spectra (Fig. 5a), the CQDs have no significant effect on the light absorption efficiency. So, the improved photoelectrochemical properties may be attributed to the improved charge separation, charge transfer or both of them. The charge transfer and separation efficiency of the photoanodes have been estimated to by using Na2SO3 as a hole scavenger as shown in Fig. 7 . The estimation method has been described in the Supporting information. Our results show that the charge separation efficiency of the Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode has been significantly improved from 41% to 49% (0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl) after being modified with CQDs, regardless of the Co-Pi catalysts (Fig. 7a). While the improvement of charge transfer efficiency is limited compared with Co-Pi catalysts (Fig. 7b). It only increased to 92% from 83% of Co-Pi/Fe2O3 or CQDs/Fe2O3 which has almost the same value of Co-Pi/Fe2O3 at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl. It seems that Co-Pi electrocatalysts mainly contribute to the charge transfer, while CQDs can not only promote the charge separation rate but also improve the charge transfer efficiency.

To further study the function of CQDs based charge bridge, SPS was also performed on the Fe2O3 and CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes. In this technique, the contact potential (photovoltage) is recorded as a function of the irradiated light wavelength [17,37]. Generally, the higher photovoltage indicates the more separated electron-hole pairs upon illumination. Fig. 7c shows the CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanodes has the higher photovoltage. It suggests that the CQDs/Fe2O3 photoanode is much more efficient in charge separation than Fe2O3 photoanode, which is consistent with the charge separation efficiency in Fig. 7a.

By measuring the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), the charge transfer process can be studied in the reaction [38]. For further quantitative analysis, the reaction process was simplified to a series connection of a RC (resistance and capacitance) circuits (Fig. 7d). Rs is the resistance of the entire PEC system. And R represents the impedance of the interface reaction between the semiconductor and the electrolyte. The R of the C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 (7.8 kΩ) photoanode was less than that of Fe2O3 (70.1 kΩ), CQDs/Fe2O3 (15.9 kΩ) and Co-Pi/Fe2O3 (8.9 kΩ), which shows that the conductivity and the electron transport of C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanodes were increased [13,39].

A possible mechanism based on the above discussion is presented in Fig. 8 . Electrons tend to diffuse to the Fe2O3 at the interface of Fe2O3 and CQDs once CQDs contact with hematite semiconductor according to the reported work function values of Fe2O3 and CQDs [16,40]. In detail, when Fe2O3 is excited by higher energy visible light, it will produce electron-hole pairs inside the semiconductor, and the photo-generated electrons were preferentially collected by the platinum electrode. While the photo-generated holes were easily transferred to the HOMO level of the CQDs [41,42]. In our case, the CQDs serve as a charge bridge between the Fe2O3 nanorods and the Co-Pi catalysts or electrolyte which will facilitate the hole transfer [13,34]. Therefore, it is possible to replace Co-Pi catalysts with other amorphous catalysts, and the role of CQDs is irreplaceable.

We have successfully synthesized C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode on FTO substrates. Compared with Co-Pi/Fe2O3 and pristine Fe2O3 photoanodes, the C-Co-Pi/Fe2O3 photoanode exhibited an excellent photocurrent due to the positive effects of CQDs on photo-generated charge separation and transfer. The CQDs are believed to have provided a charge bridge to promote holes transfer to Co-Pi catalysts or electrolyte which promotes the water oxidation reactions. The function of CQDs in such a photoanode structure is irreplaceable and this work can be demonstrative method to optimize the charge transfer kinetics between various catalysts and photoelectrode.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U1531114, 51572118 and 61404066) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (China, Grant No. lzujbky-2017-180).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.04.052.
